title: LOME as a multi-phase event
text: The Late Ordovician mass extinction is best treated as a temporally structured crisis rather than a single undifferentiated event. A workflow should therefore avoid collapsing onset, peak extinction, and recovery into one coarse bin when finer stratigraphic resolution is available.

title: Climate rate and extinction tempo
text: Recent high-resolution work emphasizes that the rate and timing of climate change are central to interpreting LOME extinction tempo. This supports workflow designs that test whether biogeographic change is synchronous with diversity loss or offset in time.

title: Redox change as heterogeneous stress
text: Redox deterioration is an important LOME stressor, but proxy syntheses and regional studies point to spatial and temporal heterogeneity. A paleobiogeographic workflow should therefore treat redox evidence as contextual and regionally variable rather than a uniform global switch.

title: Hirnantian brachiopod faunas as biogeographic evidence
text: Hirnantian brachiopod faunas provide a key lens for global LOME biogeography, but their apparent geographic coherence must be evaluated against diachroneity, environmental selectivity, facies distribution, and sampling structure.

title: South China as a heterogeneity test case
text: South China is useful for testing whether LOME brachiopod biogeographic change was spatially homogeneous. Regional analyses emphasize ecological and geographic heterogeneity within South China rather than a simple uniform response.

title: Diversity change versus biogeographic change
text: Brachiopod diversity dynamics and biogeographic reorganization are related but not identical signals. A workflow should compare richness, extinction or origination estimates, similarity structure, and spatial occupancy rather than treating any single curve as the whole evolutionary pattern.

title: Structured databases are not plain text
text: Occurrence databases such as PBDB should be accessed as structured evidence because fields for taxonomy, stratigraphy, geography, references, and collection metadata carry scientific meaning. Flattening these records into text chunks can erase relationships needed for reproducible analysis.

title: Paleogeographic reconstruction as a Skill task
text: Paleobiogeographic interpretation requires plate reconstruction tools rather than text retrieval alone. GPlates, pyGPlates, and GPlately provide a reproducible route from occurrence coordinates to deep-time spatial analyses when model choice and rotation parameters are logged.

title: Cross-taxon evidence is contextual
text: Graptolites, acritarchs, chitinozoans, corals, trilobites, cephalopods, and early vertebrates can contextualize LOME environmental and biogeographic change, but their records differ in ecology, preservation, and sampling. They should be used as independent context rather than merged uncritically into a brachiopod occurrence workflow.

title: Review questions for apparent province shifts
text: Before accepting an apparent shift in brachiopod faunal provinces, the workflow should ask whether the signal persists after synonymy cleaning, removal or flagging of open nomenclature, consistent time binning, paleogeographic reconstruction, and sampling-intensity checks.
